conversation.content:
1.Matlab option model. Download from courseworks matlab option model files BlackScholesStocks.m and BlackScholesGraph.m and put them in the same directory.
BlackScholesStocks.m contains the function that calculates the Black Scholes price for options on nondividend paying stocks.
BlackScholesGraph.m is a script that makes a graph of option price as a function of stock price.
Type at Matlab prompt >BlackScholesGraph and the script will be executed and the graph will appear.
Now modify the file BlackScholesStocks.m so that the function now calculates the price of options on stocks paying continuous dividends at a rate q.
Modify the file BlackScholesGraph.m so that it now plots graph of a call with the same parameters as before but with the dividend yield q=2.5% and the new strike price 11.5.
Submit printouts of code and graph.

Here’s the modification you need for the Black–Scholes model with a continuous dividend yield q.

The pricing formula becomes:
C = S * e^(-q*T) * N(d1) - K * e^(-r*T) * N(d2), d1 = (ln(S/K) + (r - q + 0.5 * sigma^2) * T) / (sigma * sqrt(T)), d2 = d1 - sigma * sqrt(T)

and for a put:
P = K * e^(-r*T) * N(-d2) - S * e^(-q*T) * N(-d1).

1) Modified BlackScholesStocks.m
Use this version if your original file is meant to price both calls and puts:

function price = BlackScholesStocks(S,K,r,q,sigma,T,CallPut)
% BlackScholesStocks
% Prices European call/put options on a stock paying continuous dividends.
%
% Inputs:
%   S       = stock price
%   K       = strike price
%   r       = risk-free rate
%   q       = continuous dividend yield
%   sigma   = volatility
%   T       = time to maturity
%   CallPut = 'c' for call, 'p' for put
%
% Output:
%   price   = Black-Scholes option price

d1 = (log(S./K) + (r - q + 0.5*sigma^2)*T) / (sigma*sqrt(T));
d2 = d1 - sigma*sqrt(T);

if lower(CallPut) == 'c'
    price = S.*exp(-q*T).*normcdf(d1) - K*exp(-r*T).*normcdf(d2);
elseif lower(CallPut) == 'p'
    price = K*exp(-r*T).*normcdf(-d2) - S.*exp(-q*T).*normcdf(-d1);
else
    error('CallPut must be ''c'' or ''p''.');
end

2) Modified BlackScholesGraph.m
This plots the call price as a function of stock price with:
dividend yield q = 2.5% = 0.025
strike price K = 11.5

% BlackScholesGraph
% Graph of European call price vs. stock price
% with continuous dividend yield
clear; clc;

% Parameters
K = 11.5;        % new strike price
r = 0.05;        % risk-free rate (keep same as before if different)
q = 0.025;       % dividend yield = 2.5%
sigma = 0.20;    % volatility (keep same as before if different)
T = 1;           % time to maturity (keep same as before if different)

% Range of stock prices
S = 0.01:0.1:25;

% Compute call prices
CallPrice = zeros(size(S));
for i = 1:length(S)
    CallPrice(i) = BlackScholesStocks(S(i),K,r,q,sigma,T,'c');
end

% Plot
plot(S, CallPrice, 'LineWidth', 2);
xlabel('Stock Price S');
ylabel('Call Option Price');
title('Black-Scholes Call Price with Continuous Dividend Yield');
grid on;

3) What changed
In BlackScholesStocks.m:
- you add q as an input
- replace d1 = (ln(S/K) + (r + 0.5 * sigma^2) * T) / (sigma * sqrt(T)) with d1 = (ln(S/K) + (r - q + 0.5 * sigma^2) * T) / (sigma * sqrt(T))
- multiply the stock term by e^(-q*T)
So the call changes from: S * N(d1) - K * e^(-r*T) * N(d2) to: S * e^(-q*T) * N(d1) - K * e^(-r*T) * N(d2)

4) Important note
Your instructor says “same parameters as before,” so if the original BlackScholesGraph.m already had values for r, sigma, T, or the stock-price range, keep those the same and only change:
- K to 11.5
- add q = 0.025
- update the function call so it passes q

5) If your original function is call-only
Sometimes the original file only prices calls. Then BlackScholesStocks.m can be even simpler:

function price = BlackScholesStocks(S,K,r,q,sigma,T)
% Prices a European call option on a dividend-paying stock
d1 = (log(S./K) + (r - q + 0.5*sigma^2)*T) / (sigma*sqrt(T));
d2 = d1 - sigma*sqrt(T);
price = S.*exp(-q*T).*normcdf(d1) - K*exp(-r*T).*normcdf(d2);

and then in the graph script:
CallPrice(i) = BlackScholesStocks(S(i),K,r,q,sigma,T);

If you paste your original two MATLAB files, I’ll rewrite them in exactly the same style as your class version.
